exemplar.title:
Impact of Color Red Text On Pre-University Teachers Ability to Recall
Information

detailedExemplarData.title:
Impact of Color Red Text On Pre-University Teachers Ability to Recall
Information

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The aim is clearly stated and its relevance is well explained for cognitive and classroom applications.
Two theories/models are described and explicitly linked to the investigation rationale.
Hypotheses include both null and experimental forms with IV and DV operationalised.
weaknesses: Minor imprecision in stating the aim (participant group/details omitted).
Links between the MultiâStore Model stages and the recall task could be tighter.
ColourâinâContext theory implications for memory recall remain somewhat vague.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction consistently meets the 3â4 band across all strands: the aim, theory description/link, and hypothesis/variables are all present and largely well explained. However, small lapses in wording, depth of theoretical linkage, and precision justify a solid midâband score of 4 rather than the top mark.

strengths: The betweenâsubjects design is explained and justified for controlling comparative issues.
Convenience sampling is described with participant demographics and a noted limitation on generalisability.
weaknesses: Controlled variables and materials are listed but lack explanation of their selection or control rationale.
No power analysis or deeper mitigation strategies for sampling bias are provided.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the research design and sampling reach the explanatory level (3â4 band), the materials/variables strand remains at the descriptive level (1â2 band). Overall, the exploration section best fits a score of 2, reflecting mixed performance with insufficient depth in control rationale.

strengths: Descriptive (mean, SD, median, mode) and inferential (oneâtailed MannâWhitney U) statistics are appropriately applied and accurately interpreted.
Findings are clearly stated and linked to the hypothesis, with correct rejection of the null.
weaknesses: No graphical presentation is provided; only a table appears instead of a graph addressing the hypothesis.
Minor formatting errors in pâvalue notation and statement of significance thresholds.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis and interpretation fully meet the 3â4 band, bordering on 5â6, but the complete absence of a proper graph drags the graphical presentation strand to the 1â2 band. A midâband score of 3 fairly reflects these strengths and gaps.

strengths: Findings are discussed in reference to the Colour-in-Context theory.
Relevant strengths and limitations of the investigation are identified and linked to the hypothesis.
Modifications are specific and connected to improving generalisability and validity.
weaknesses: Discussion is fairly brief and descriptive rather than deeply analytical.
Modifications lack explicit mapping to each limitation for full justification.
Depth of critique on methodological weaknesses is limited.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each evaluation strand sits squarely in the 3â4 band but is only modestly developed. The section meets criteria for relevance and linkage but lacks analytical depth and full justification of improvements, warranting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The submission references a âfigureâ but only a table is visible and **no graph is provided in the excerpt**. Assuming the absence of a proper graph, the criterion reaches only the 1-2 band for choosing a tabular technique that does not address the hypothesis graphically. A minimal score of 1 is therefore appropriate.

The student **clearly states the statistical findings** (significant difference, p < .05) and **interprets them in relation to the hypothesis**, concluding that the null is rejected and the experimental hypothesis supported. Interpretation is linked to variability data and SDs, satisfying the 5-6 band; slight over-generalisation keeps the score at 5.

The hypothesis section **states a null and an experimental hypothesis**. The IV (text colour: red vs black) and DV (recall score) are both **clearly operationalised**âscore is quantified through a five-question test, and colour is specified precisely. This meets the 5-6 band. Minor wording issues ("amount of information" instead of explicit âscore out of 5â) slightly reduce precision, so 5 is awarded.

Convenience sampling is **identified and its practicality explained**; participant characteristics (age, gender, cultural background, colour-vision screening) are provided and the limitation of generalisability is noted. This constitutes an explanation rather than mere description, placing it in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

The student **describes two theories/models** (Multi-store model of memory and Colour-in-Context theory) with key features outlined. They then **explicitly link the Colour-in-Context theory to the present investigation**, arguing that colour carries psychological meaning that may affect memory performance. Because the model is both described and connected to the study rationale, the work satisfies the 5-6 band; small conceptual imprecision justifies awarding 5 rather than 6.

The conclusion relates findings to the Colour-in-Context theory, but the **discussion is brief and largely descriptive** rather than deeply analytical. Still, there is a clear reference to background theory, placing it in the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

**The aim is clearly stated** (to discover whether red or black text influences teachersâ recall) and is followed by a paragraph that **explicitly explains why this question is important** for understanding colour effects on cognition and for real-world applications such as classroom practice. This explanatory discussion goes beyond merely identifying relevance, therefore fitting the 5-6 band. Minor lapses in wording do not detract from an otherwise complete explanation, so a score of 5 is warranted within the top band.

The student lists ethical strength and experimental control, and identifies a limitation (restricted generalisability). Each point is **described and linked to the investigation**, meeting the 3-4 band. Limited depth and absence of methodological critique place it at 3.

Controlled variables (length and wording of text) and materials (consent form, coloured questionnaires) are **listed** but the rationale for their selection/control is not really explained. Because this is description without explanatory depth, the work fits the 1-2 band; both elements are present, so the top of that band (2) is given.

Several **specific modifications** (broader sample, different hues, replication) are provided and are **directly tied to the stated limitation of generalisability and to strengthening validity**. The justifications are adequate but not fully developed, so the work sits comfortably in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

The student **explains** the choice of a between-subjects two-group design and justifies how it controls comparison issues. This goes beyond description and thus fits the 3-4 band; the explanation is brief and lacks depth about control of confounds, so 3 is appropriate.

Appropriate **descriptive statistics (mean, SD, median, mode)** and an **appropriate inferential test (one-tailed Mann-Whitney U)** are used. The critical value, U, z-score and p-value are reported and interpreted correctly, with no evident computational errors. This accurate application of both descriptive and inferential statistics satisfies the 5-6 band; minor formatting issues justify a 5.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear description of the three stores in the multi-store model, establishing a solid theoretical foundation.
The discussion of decoding, comprehension and storing is detailed but lacks explicit connection to the recall task; link these processes directly to the memory test.
Colour-in-Context theory is described, but its specific implications for memory recall are vague; clarify how redâs psychological associations might affect performance.
The aim statement ends mid-sentence and omits full operational details; ensure it clearly specifies participant group, IV and DV.
The opening paragraph in the exploration section repeats the aim with informal phrasing; tighten language and focus on transitions from theory to method.
The independent variable (âtext color: red or blackâ) is clearly identified and operationalised, enhancing experimental clarity.
Describing the DV as âamount of informationâ is too vague; specify it precisely as ânumber of correct items recalled (score out of 5)â.
The experimental hypothesis is directional, consistent with theory, and clearly predicts lower redâtext recall versus blackâtext recall.
Research design is explained and justified for between-subjects comparison; expand on random assignment procedures to show how selection bias is minimized.
Convenience sampling is acknowledged but introduces bias; discuss how this choice could impact internal validity and suggest mitigation strategies.
Participant demographics (age range, gender, background, colour-vision screening) are comprehensively detailed, supporting transparency about the sample.
Materials are listed (consent form, questionnaire) but lack rationale; explain how each choice controls for confounds and ensures consistency.
The procedural steps are clearly outlined in sequence, enhancing reproducibility and clarity about timing and debriefing.
Ethical procedures (consent, confidentiality, right to withdraw) are thoroughly addressed and reflect strong adherence to ethical standards.
Justification for using the mean is given but omits rationale for small, ordinal-like data; discuss why mean was chosen over median or mode.
No graphical presentation is provided, only a table; include a graph (e.g., boxplot or bar chart) to visually address the hypothesis.
The dual mode values (4,2) are noted but their implication for variability is unclear; clarify why two modes arise and what this indicates.
The p-value notation â05â and significance wording are imprecise; correct to âp = 0.0409, p < 0.05â and specify exact values.
Ethical rigor and laboratory control are well articulated, underscoring strong internal validity and procedural integrity.
Generalizability limitation is acknowledged for non-teachers; deepen by discussing how age, culture or educational level might further affect recall.
Suggested modifications are relevant but lack explicit linkage to each stated limitation; map each modification directly to its corresponding limitation.
The conclusion misstates the significance threshold as âp â¤ 0.5â; correct to âp â¤ 0.05â to accurately reflect statistical significance.
Findings are effectively discussed in light of Colour-in-Context theory, linking cognitive data to psychological meaning of black versus red text.
Reference entries vary in format and completeness; standardize citations consistently to a recognized style (e.g., APA 7th edition).
Sample size rationale is missing; consider performing a power analysis to justify why 20 teachers is sufficient to detect your hypothesized effect.
